mission.title: Install and Configure Valkey
mission.description: Valkey is a high-performance distributed key-value database optimized for caching and real-time workloads. It is suitable for applications requiring fast data access and supports various data structures and plugins. Key trade-offs include the need for careful configuration and management of resources to ensure optimal performance.

steps.title: Add the Valkey Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Valkey Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add valkey https://github.com/valkey-io/valkey/releases/download/v9.0.3/valkey-9.0.3.tgz

```

> Ensure you are using the correct URL for the Helm chart.

steps.title: Install Valkey CRDs
steps.description:
This step installs the Custom Resource Definitions required for Valkey to function properly.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://github.com/valkey-io/valkey/releases/download/v9.0.3/valkey-crds.yaml

```

> Ensure the CRDs are installed before deploying the main application.

steps.title: Install Valkey using Helm
steps.description:
This step installs the Valkey application in the specified namespace using Helm.

```bash

helm install valkey valkey/valkey --namespace valkey --version 9.0.3

```

> Replace 'valkey' with your desired release name.

steps.title: Create an example Valkey Custom Resource
steps.description:
This step creates a sample Custom Resource to deploy an instance of Valkey.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: valkey.io/v1
kind: Valkey
metadata:
  name: example-valkey
spec:
  replicas: 3
EOF

```

> This example creates a Valkey instance with 3 replicas.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: valkey.io/v1
kind: Valkey
metadata:
  name: example-valkey
spec:
  replicas: 3
EOF

steps.title: Verify Valkey is running
steps.description:
This step checks the status of the Valkey deployment to ensure it is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n valkey

```

> You should see the Valkey pods in a Running state.

uninstall.title: Uninstall Valkey
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Valkey deployment and associated resources. Be aware that this will result in data loss.

```bash

helm uninstall valkey --namespace valkey

kubectl delete namespace valkey

```

> This command will remove the Valkey release.
> This command will delete the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Valkey
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your data. This step will upgrade Valkey to the latest version.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n valkey -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}' | xargs -I {} kubectl drain {} --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

helm upgrade valkey valkey/valkey --namespace valkey --version 9.0.3

kubectl get pods -n valkey

```

> Drain the nodes running Valkey.
> Upgrade to the specified version.
> Verify that the pods are running the new version.
commands: kubectl get pods -n valkey -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}' | xargs -I {} kubectl drain {} --ignore-daemonsets --delete-local-data

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to Valkey server'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Valkey server may not be running or is misconfigured.

**Fix:**
Check the pod status with 'kubectl get pods -n valkey' and review logs with 'kubectl logs <pod-name> -n valkey'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient resources to schedule pod'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The cluster may not have enough resources available.

**Fix:**
Scale up your cluster or adjust resource requests in your Valkey deployment.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Valkey pod is in CrashLoopBackOff'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Valkey pod may be crashing due to misconfiguration or resource limits.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs for errors and adjust the configuration as necessary.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Custom Resource not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Valkey Custom Resource Definition may not be installed.

**Fix:**
Reinstall the CRDs using 'kubectl apply -f https://github.com/valkey-io/valkey/releases/download/v9.0.3/valkey-crds.yaml'.

versionNotes.changes: Introduced enhanced performance optimizations and support for new data structures.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated certain legacy APIs for improved efficiency.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from version 8.x, review the migration guide for changes in configuration.

resolution.summary: You should see the Valkey pods running with the status 'Running' when executing 'kubectl get pods -n valkey'.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: valkey.io/v1
kind: Valkey
metadata:
  name: example-valkey
spec:
  replicas: 3
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster set up and accessible.
